content:
# Top 5 posts from r/MovieSuggestions day

## 1. The most random movies to have R-rated sex scenes

**Score:** 125
**Body:** There are certain genres of films, usually thrillers, horrors, romance and sometimes broad comedy, where you aren't surprised by a sex scene if it's an R-rated movie

But which are some films that you watched, that didn't at all give off the vibe of having sex in it, and then suddenly boom - naked people are boning. 

So obviously "random" is a very subjective thing, and the reason as to why you were surprised will be unique to you - but try to focus on movies that didn't seem to be sex-forward in their marketing, and/or weren't the kind of films that usually have explicit content in it





### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Team America
  - I promise. I will never die!
2. Crank (2006)
Jason Statham plays this assassin who must keep his adrenaline pumping to survive. To get his heart pumping, Statham's character decides to have public sex with his girlfriend in the middle of a crowded Chinatown.

I burst out laughing, lol.
  - Crank 2 features a sex scene in the dirt of a horse track, during a race! Horses running by and all. Bonus cameo from Chester Bennington appallingly bearing witness. One of the few sequels I feel surpass the original.
3. Thundergun. Definitely wasn’t expecting that dong
4. Oppenheimer
5. MacGruber has two and man, are they both memorable!
6. Not a movie but had a jump scare at the end of episode 5 (?) of The Chair Company.
7. Love Actually. Every Christmas, I have to remind my wife that, no, the movie that includes a couple shooting hardcore pornography is not family friendly.
  - My teenage daughter watched it on an airplane on a flight home when it came out. Insisted her mother and I rent it and watch it with her. The airplane cut did not have the porn part. Awkward!

## 2. Bugonia (2025) - If you liked '10 Cloverfield Lane' but wished it was directed by a madman

**Score:** 65
**Body:** I just finished *Bugonia* and I wanted to recommend it to anyone who likes contained thrillers.

It’s basically a kidnapping movie where two conspiracy theorists hold a pharmaceutical CEO hostage because they think she’s an alien. The premise sounds silly, but it plays it surprisingly straight with just enough dark comedy to make it uncomfortable.

It hits that same sweet spot as *Misery* or *10 Cloverfield Lane*, where you are stuck in a room with crazy people, but you start wondering if maybe just maybe they are right. Definitely worth a watch if you want something tense but weird.

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I also loved Bugonia and your description is spot-on
2. With a small role by classically trained actor, Stavros Halkias. Excellent flick.
  - I felt like that subplot was kind of unnecessary and weird to force into the plot. There were tons of reasons a cop would be around other than THAT
3. I loved this weird ass movie!
4. Thanks will try it
5. This sounds like exactly my kind of “trapped in a room with unhinged people” stress-watch lol. I love when a thriller makes you go ok wait what if they’re right for like 10 seconds. The dark comedy angle makes it even creepier to me. I’m adding it to my list for a night when I wanna feel uncomfortable on purpose.
6. I liked the overall message but I found the characters just didn't have that larger than life quality I wanted from a movie with only 3 main roles. The plot felt pretty standard and even the ending was predictable. I know it's a remake, but it's obvious if you've seen that other Lanthimos movie >!Kinds of Kindness!<
7. I watched the original Save the Green Planet (2003) a few months ago for the first time and just realized it got remade. I'm on the fence about watching that new movie since I'm not a fan of most remakes, tbh.
  - I understand, but do give Bugonia a watch.
8. Saw preview looks good!

## 3. Just watched Nightcrawler (2014) and it completely pulled me in

**Score:** 51
**Body:** It kept me engaged from start to finish. The storytelling was intense, the pacing never dipped, and Jake Gyllenhaal’s performance made the character unsettling in the best way. Every scene felt purposeful, and the ending lingered with me long after it ended.

I really enjoy films like this dark, psychological, and thought-provoking.  
If you know movies with a similar tone or impact, I’d love to hear your recommendations.

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Nocturnal Animals (2016) also starring Jake G

In The Cut (2003)

Zodiac (2007)
2. "A Clockwork Orange" (1971) - Another chilling portrayal of a pathological young fellow. Malcolm McDowell as Alex is a pure cinema icon.

"Barry Lyndon" (1975) - This dude straight hustling, he a prick though.

"Taxi Driver" (1976) - Robert De Niro's portrayal of Travis Bickle is fundamental. Love the cameo from Scorsese. The night time cruises with Bernard Hermann's haunting score reflect on more than just what runs down the gutters.

"The Cell" (2000) - I love this flick. Vincent D'Onofrio rains the trauma down in this bleak, virulent machine of toxicity. Really, really "consuming" performance.
3. that's part one of the reddit trio. 

you now want to watch part two of the reddit trio, Whiplash.
  - 3. Biodome
4. Masterpiece
5. Try Lurker. Similar themes with a more modern setting. Although if you’re not tapped in to groupie culture it may not resonate as much.
6. It's so good I never want to watch it again. *Unsettling* doesn't do Gyllenhaal's performance justice.
7. Love this movie
8. Ace in the Hole (1951) was an inspiration to Nightcrawler. [Here's an interview with Nightcrawler filmmaker, Dan Gilroy](https://www.indiewire.com/features/general/interview-nightcrawler-director-dan-gilroy-talks-jake-gyllenhaal-robert-elswit-sociopaths-270834/). He mentions the following films about media: 

* Ace in the Hole (1951)
* Network (1976)
* Broadcast News (1987)
* To Die For (1995)
9. I'd say Whiplash. Made me feels the same way!

## 4. Movies with unintentional eerie undertones

**Score:** 39
**Body:** I'm talking about movies that aren't supposed to be horror, but you just can't help getting an eerie feeling afterwards. For example; I was watching the 1993 the secret garden some days ago for the nostalgic impact, don't get me wrong, it's whimsical and all, but something just feels off. 


### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I never liked the Original Wizard of Oz and found it to be very eerie as a child. It kind of makes sense as every actor on that set suffered in some way or another.
2. Does the film Kids count?  JFC, that movie is one hell of a warning, especially considering the rise in Syphilis.
  - Gummo and Trash Humpers as well
3. Sound of Music. 

My cousin recently asked what I thought might be the best World War II movie is (because I’m a WWII buff and was currently watching the Man in the High Castle for the second time). Never once had I even really put it in the category, but without thinking I said The Sound of Music. 

He, being a film maker (Japan, Korea, USA), was as surprised as I was. We acknowledged it *was* about WWII. 

And that’s why it’s eerie, to me. Because I watched over and over as a kid, we couldn’t wait for it to play each year. My kids watched it. My grandkids have seen it. 

Until that day… it was a family movie kids watched. Kids generally enjoy. 

Yet, it really wasn’t was it? The movie itself is what OP asked for, it’s a family story; happy on the outside with ups and downs and laughter and kindness, music, coming alive, coming of age, a wedding, a moral! The truth is they were *living in a scary dark time* where they were having to watch their backs and worry about serious things until they eventually had no choice but to escape. Let’s not fool ourselves, that escape was incredibly dangerous, not a sure thing, probable death penalty without a trial was a strong possibility; mountains aren’t easy to climb. People you meet might turn you over. Shoot, traveling with so many people… on and on how dark this story is. 

That they got away and survived and lived to tell about it is a real happy ending—in a world where millions of others either didn’t live to see one and millions others had to live monstrous tortured lives for years wondering daily if it’s for naught. 

Anyway. I could go on and on. I’ve always thought of it as such a heart warming story about family and strength and the beauty of art. And it is. Yet… eerier and eerie the more I keep thinking about it.
  - The Best Years of Our Lives is an excellent WWII film if you haven’t seen it yet.
4. Chitty Chitty Bang Bang
5. Boogie Nights
6. The Truman Show.

I don’t know if eerie is quite the right word. But the more you think about it, the more horrifying it gets. Everyone, lying to this guy’s face. 24 hours a day, 7 days a week. His wife is fake, his best friend is fake. His parents are liars.

It’s framed as a triumphant tale of perseverance, but really it’s sad. All the people in the world watching this show, and no one stops it. They all just watch.
7. They Live (1988) - action movie & the threat is made out to be more evil than scary. When you really think about it, it’s horrifying and very eerie
8. All Dogs Go to Heaven. Saw it in the theater as a little kid and it always left me in shambles. Come to find out that the little girl in the film is voiced by Judith Barsi who was horribly abused by her father and was murdered by him just before the film’s voice overs were concluded. You can hear the turmoil and absolute crushing sadness in Burt Reynolds voice overs as some of the last days of him recording were directly after Judith and her mother were murdered.

## 5. I'm tired of recent Hollywood movies. I need good movies to watch.

**Score:** 38
**Body:** ​After a long run of disappointing major Hollywood releases this year, I'm finding that there's no going back from international, independent cinema. I'm actively seeking high-quality, impactful films that deliver a profound, lasting impact, the kind of movie that genuinely stays with you forever.

*​My Criteria (Flexible!)*

*​Genre:* Anything is welcome! (Drama, Sci-Fi, Crime, Arthouse, etc.)

*​Safety Note:* Psychological thrillers are great, but please avoid explicit horror (guts, excessive torture/gore like Saw).

*​Origin:* Any Country. Any Language. The more international, the better.

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Sentimental Value (Norway, 2025) was phenomenal and so well acted. Highly recommended.
2. Secret Sunshine is a Korean drama/thriller from 2007 that I've always found hauntingly beautiful and deeply affecting. It's about a young, widowed piano teacher and her son who move from the big city to a small, more rural area of Korea. It's a very emotional film!
  - I love Korean thrillers but I didn't know this one! Added to my list 🙌🏻
3. A few of my favorite foreign / non Hollywood films from the 60s and 70s:

'Investigation of a Citizen Above Suspicion' (1970) is a Kafka-esque crime thriller about a newly promoted chief of police who kills his girlfriend and deliberately incriminates himself multiple times over the course of the investigation just to test the limits of his power and authority. It's a fantastic film, and Gian Maria Volonte is a force of nature in it.

'Le Trou' (1960) is a French prison escape film based on real events. It's about a group of five men who are clandestinely tunneling out of prison. A film that takes place just in one cell and one small tunnel has no right being so tense, but it is. It's a fantastic film.

'Wake in Fright' (1971) is a journey to the pit of despair via alcohol, gambling, and toxic masculinity in a remote Australian town where there is nothing else to. It's a harrowing film, and the amount of alcohol that is drank will make you feel sick! It's an incredible film.
4. Watch the first two Dogme 95 movies. (The Celebration and The Idiots.) If you want a break from Hollywood films, those are great for it. Dogme 95 was a Danish film movement that felt that same way about the rules of Hollywood so they made their own set of rules to combat that
5. City of God is easily the best movie of all time imo
6. I don't know about staying with you forever, but Madeo (2009) and Contratiempo (2016) are near 10s out of 10 for me

Sincerily, someone who loves psuchological thrillers

>!Avoid the "Contratiempo" remakes btw, which are 3 as far as I know!<
7. it was just an accident
8. Double Indemnity (1944)

In Cold Blood (1967)

Days of Being Wild (1990)

Barry Lyndon (1975)

Persona (1966)

Badlands (1973)

The Misfits (1961)

Fat City (1972)

3 Women (1977)

When a Woman Ascends the Stairs (1960)

Girl with Suitcase (1961)
9. Yeah dude same

# Top 5 posts from r/moviecritic day

## 1. Male characters who have great chemistry with each other. I’ll start

**Score:** 2272
**Body:** For me it's Russell and Ryan from Nice guys.
They have pretty impressive chemistry.

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. The guys from In Bruges.
  - They are also the same guys in "The Banshees of Inisherin". Check the film out if you haven't. It's phenomenal.
    - Colin Farrell and Brendan Gleeson.

Love both movies
2. Riggs and Murtagh
3. Ryan gosling and Steve carrell
  - Ryan and anyone, really lol. that man could have chemistry with a sheet.
4. Indiana Jones & his dad

Michael J Fox & Christopher Llyod
5. Chris Tucker and Jackie Chan
6. Marty and Rust True Detective
7. The Other Guys

## 2. What's the worst death scene directed by a highly decorated filmmaker?

**Score:** 831

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Yes.. Talia al Ghul’s death is infamous, stiff, rushed, and unintentionally funny.
  - If I was her people, I’d be pretty pissed at Nolan for using this take.
2. I think Nolan sometimes rushes through. Like in the fight scenes.
  - The fight scenes are by far the worst part of the dark knight trilogy

Probably the only real solid ones are the early League of Shadow scenes at the start.
3. Oscar in Threat Level Midnight. Michael Scott left the part when he was blinking while dead.
  - It’s all dwigt’s fault.
4. That death scene cracked me up
5. Hicks and Newt in Alien 3
  - More like complete lack of a death scene, there. They are killed off-screen and the audience finds out their ultimate fate along with Ripley later. Still pisses me off. Then they have the gall to do the same thing with Elizabeth Shaw in Covenant.
6. Uncle Ben's death in the Amazing Spiderman is pretty awful too

## 3. Anyone wish Eric Bana should have been a bigger star?

**Score:** 469
**Body:** I first saw him in Hulk and loved the film and his performance. (Yes I know it’s not exactly a loved film) but I really felt like he was going to be a bigger star than he was. His performance in Troy was great. Being the hero and having that spectacular sword fight, then starring in Munich and Black Hawk Down. A few years I did see Chopper. Which again was a pretty good film. But other than that… not much. Maybe it was Hulk not being a big hit like they hoped is why he never took off? Anyways, what are your thoughts?

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Eric Bana played Bruce Bana
  - Bruce Bana sounds like he woulda been buds with Steve and Bucky
2. I liked him as Hector in Troy.
  - He was great in Blackhawk down too.
    - Agreed

“Well this is my safety sir”, such a great scene haha
3. Every time I see Munich I reflexively think "this is one of the best movies I've ever watched!"  He's a huge part of that.  I wonder why he doesn't get cast much?
4. He killed it in Blackhawk Down, but there were dozens of stellar performances in that film so it's difficult for any single actor to stand out.


He's still got a lot of miles left on him too. He did thaf Netflix series a while back and it did so well they instantly green lit a second season. 
5. He was pretty big, and he was like my second or third favorite Romulan, but I do wish he'd do more stuff
6. I think he's considered a big big star in Australia, his home country. Chopper is what put him on the map iirc
  - Neville fucking Bartos!

## 4. What's a movie that you hear people talking crap about but you genuinely like, for me it's Smoking Aces

**Score:** 382
**Body:** I really liked it, now mind you I was high the first time I watched it so the elevator scene really felt real to me but overall I thought it was a really good movie.

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Smokin Aces is great.
  - And Lucky number Slevin. Good early 2000 action
  - Like who said it was a bad movie
2. Crank. It’s not high art or anything but it’s a good hour and a half to turn the brain off and enjoy stupid action.
  - Crank and crank 2 high voltage are amazing! I still have the ringtone for one of my friends
3. I love Smoking Aces. The cast is stellar and the over the top violence and story are awesome.
4. RocknRolla
5. I'm with you on Smokin' Aces. It's just a fun movie. Nothing too deep. Just silly fun.
6. The island with Ewan Mccgregor and Scarlet Johanson.
7. Ah, the movie me and all me high school friends wanted to see because Ari Gold was in it. Thats all I remember about it and I watched it

## 5. Secrete of The Ooze

**Score:** 228
**Body:** This movie was awesome.

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I quote this movie to my toddler son all the time. 

- When grocery shopping: “Pizza’s okay”

- When stopping by dunkin before work:
“First, we must observe the ancient ritual of the... traditional pre-fight donut.”

- After getting bad customer service:
“Boy, whatever happened to "service with a smile"?”

The first two TMNT movies are endlessly quotable.
  - They’re BABIES!!!
    - “Muh…muh…momma!?”
2. Yeah, a little too Raph!
3. Go ninja, go ninja, GO

Ten flips now!
4. It’s not as dark as the first one but it’s still pretty good
  - Yeah, the turtles never use their weapons in Part 2 lol
5. I got my 4k set last night and today is my last day of work for the year. I am beyond hyped and trying to plan out what to eat/drink for my marathon
6. You can't make it through the opening without getting a slice. 
7. "Secreting ooze" is too much information. The movie in question was fun but inferior to it's predecessor.

# Top 5 posts from r/TrueFilm day

## 1. In Bugonia, Lanthimos traps the audience inside the logic of a conspiracy theory until it almost starts making sense

**Score:** 153
**Body:** What struck me most about *Bugonia* is how Lanthimos approaches the insane premise with a terrifyingly calm precision. In many thrillers involving conspiracy theorists, the characters are portrayed as manic or unhinged, constantly signalling to the audience that they are detached from reality.

Plemons does something far more disturbing here, he plays the kidnapper like a bureaucrat. There is a specific scene in the first act where he explains the rules of the interrogation to Emma Stone’s character. He speaks with the weary patience of a man who believes he is the only reasonable person in the room. He treats the kidnapping not as a crime, but as a necessary administrative procedure to save the world.

This creates a unique tension because the film validates the *internal* logic of the conspiracy. By refusing to break the claustrophobia of the basement setting, the audience is forced to live entirely within Plemons’ framework. We stop looking for an escape and start analyzing the evidence right alongside him.

The film seems less interested in the question of "Is she an alien?" and more focused on the mechanism of belief itself how quickly violence becomes justifiable once you accept a single false axiom. It’s a brutal examination of how shared delusion operates, anchored by a performance that refuses to give the audience the comfort of a clear villain.

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. If you go down the rabbit hole, you'll start believing crazy things like the army dumped chemicals into St Louis to study how people got sick. Or that intelligence agencies traffick kids in pedo sex rings to create kompromat on billionaires and scientists to control them. That Israel bombed US ships to try to bring them into their wars. Or that right wing and left wing politics are just kayfabe theater fed to us to keep us feeling like we have some kind of control when we lost it all a long time ago.

The film captures the desperate hopeless feeling of foolish conspiracy theorists begging to be taken seriously when they know they're right, but can't get anyone to listen. Thank God they aren't right about anything; what would we even do if our leadership were overtaken by LLM speak CEOs feeding us outrage dopamine nonsense to distract us from the fact they're poisoning our food, our minds and our planet? How would one even ring the alarm bell on that without sounding like a lunatic?
  - You comment is upvoted but you did not receive any intelligent comment so I feel obligated to tell you, you are dead right. I don't like conspirationists. One of them killed my uncle and a couple of other people with his truck a couple of years ago. it has destroyed one side of my family during covid. They are usuallu uneducated people who discover too late in their life that this world is built on injustice. What I usually do with conspirationists that try to convince me chemtrails are bad is that I agree with them and I start to explain what happened centuries ago when some people decided that the lands would be private, Basically I try to explain capitalisme and liberalism. Real conspiracies exist in this world but they are just too huge that the average conspirationist does have the knowledge of how the world really works to truly understand all the implications. 

But I do share their frustration. And I am not alone. A lot of people clearly see how is world is going to shit. And no matter how much we yell, the vast majority does not care. Even on reddit the kind of comment I am making will probably be downvoted in the end and the intellecutal level is way above average on this site.  Imagine how it in the real world now.  

It's a great film imo.
    - As usual, our language limits us. And conspicuous spending is a filter for how seriously we take someone's opinion.

"Conspirationalists" are a wide variety of people. From the very sane to the insane. We typically write them off based on their voting block or their wealth level. The film does a great job of showing how both characters have the same workout routine, read the same things, are articulate, etc. We instinctively dismiss the rural one for veblen reasons. And we assume he's our political enemy, despite him saying he identified as leftist at one point. 

Our political climate is full of leftists that love SpaceX. Right wingers that love their Hispanic undocumented coworkers, etc. But we lump everyone into easily digestible groups.

While we fight, there is absolutely an overarching conspiracy by the wealthy that is successfully robbing us blind. And we all agree on it.
2. I agree. Teddy is a character who's life has gone upside down with traumatic events one after another. He has to make sense of it somehow. His relationship with is complicated. Cause while he clearly loves him and believes what he's doing is the right thing but is ultimately taking advantage of his cousin. 

I think the film does make you question whether humanity is worth saving. Which I think has lead to ppl critiquing the film as being pessimistic. But I think its discussion worth having.
3. >He speaks with the weary patience of a man who believes he is the only reasonable person in the room.

This is in fact a very typical way for conspiracy theorists to talk. They believe that their evidence is irrefutable and, because they’ve spent far more time thinking about and researching their issue than the average person, they get frustrated when others aren’t up to speed on their conspiracy lore. I’ve experienced this talking to climate change deniers and election truthers.

Personally, I did not “start analyzing the evidence right alongside” Plemons—because his “evidence” is so clearly nonsensical. The first indications that Stone is actually an alien are her own actions after Plemons goes off to deal with the cop. Part of the joke of the film is that Plemons ends up being largely correct about the Andromedans mostly by luck, and in a way that is of utterly no use to him in the end. The idea that online conspiracy theorists could somehow piece together an accurate portrait of an alien conspiracy is *funny*. When we see that the Andromedans’ ship corresponds exactly to Plemons’ research, the reaction isn’t, “wow, he was right!” it’s laughter at the absurdity of it—because *there’s simply no way he could have known*—and somehow knowing resulted in nothing good for him.

I do agree with your conclusion that the film is focused on

>the mechanism of belief itself how quickly violence becomes justifiable once you accept a single false axiom. It’s a brutal examination of how shared delusion operates
  - The great part is how this message flips onto the Andromedans themselves, as the Queen decides to pull the plug on all of humanity, deciding we are all hopeless after dealing with Teddy's torture. Once she is put through this trauma, much like Teddy's whole life has been, she has made up her mind. This turn of events changes the movie's message from humans are flawed, to a true dogma that exists in the universe that affects all living things.
4. I think the political side of the film has been underrated generally. Two - I apologise - alienating ideologies with Don as the ordinary guy in the middle who can't see a way out. Together with the theory v praxis side of OBAA (ie. the Benicio del Toro character is the only genuinely political character in the movie), this seems to me to be a pretty accurate picture of where we are at the moment. It's also interesting that both films are comedies of course, otherwise how do you deal with any of this.
5. I think you make very interesting points.. After a while, it really seems less and less important whether she would turn out to be an alien or not, because either way we are being controlled and killed by a corrupted system. 

The twist really threw me off at first and I felt like it invalidated the narrative, but maybe that's just because it comes as such a tongue-in-cheek punchline that we are seduced into the conclusion that it was all a big joke. But then the film lingers on your mind, and makes you want to infuse it with another, better, more conclusive narrative, not unlike another conspiracy theorist. And in the end, perhaps the answers are not for us to be discovered and understood, just endured.
6. It wasn't a conspiracy theory though, it was a conspiracy and made perfect sense within the logic of the film's world. The deeper subtext of the film is that because Plemons was from the lower classes and deprived of a comprehensive education, his obvious intellectual brilliance was only nurtured by his own curiosity and moral convictions that led him to fringe online communities instead of mainstream science. He was the only man who could have saved the world, and actually came close on his own, but imagine if his genius was recognized early by his society and he was given the chance to rise to his full capacities. The overall message is an endorsement for true meritocracy.
7. You sound susceptible. His treatment and manipulation of his cousin was cruel and sickening. I know how the story unfolds, so sure, he was “right” about the aliens, but he was still a creep.

## 2. There Will Be Blood — Daniel's backstory

**Score:** 46
**Body:** In the original script, Daniel tells Henry that his "cock doesn't work". This makes sense. Daniel isn't this way just because he wants money—people with his sort of imperative are soothing a wound, replacing something they've lost. He does not choose solitude, but sees it as the only path forward.

I think PTA's choice to omit this information is strong. The film embraces Daniel's point of view, including the way he buries his pain as deeply as possible. Of course, it is still always there. He can't hide or escape it. It defines him.

To dig deeper, there are some hints at *why* he is impotent. At the beginning of the scene by the fire with Henry, Daniel asks "what did my mother know?" and Henry says "I don't know if she knew and looked the other way or if she never knew". I'll take a guess and say this is about Ernest. Perhaps it is about him cheating on Daniel's mother, having children with other women, as Henry seems to be talking about his own childhood. Or some kind of  abuse he inflicted on his children or other members of his family. Of course, Daniel and Henry are not discussing sexual abuse inflicted on Daniel. Had that transpired, Daniel would not let it rise to the surface or reveal that pain, at least not in that moment. At most, he'll talk about what his father did to *others*. In any case, a deep wound like that could explain his impotence and who he is overall, and with how things turned out, he simply never let it come out over the course of his life, carrying it with him all the way through. The closest he'll get is saying he "doesn't get along" with his Ernest.

I love the moment in that scene when Henry asks about his wife and Daniel says "I don't want to talk about those things" and then immediately goes back to "I see the worst in people Henry" (a strange thing to say almost boastfully). In real-time, you see how that unprocessed grief, that he doesn't want to touch or explore, feeds directly into his attitude toward life, where hatred and selfishness are desirable, because they allow him to reach the top, the only thing he feels can replace the loss of his sexuality. There is a depressing simplicity to how that pain gets transmuted into the single-minded drive that defines him.

Are there any other moments in the film that reveal the roots of his character, what made him who he is?

### Top 3 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. The idea of impotence isn't in the book, so this is all PTA. The most important thing is that he removed it from the story he presented, which is to say it's not something to consider when viewing the film.

Is it interesting? Yes, and it can be fun to discuss, so please don't misconstrue my point here.

If we considered everything edited out of a film or book, then it negates the purpose of editing and the presentation of a finished piece. I don't think it's fair to do such a thing to a piece of art.
  - "Unfair" is an overdramatic term. I believe what I'm saying can be gleaned, though perhaps not definitively, from the film without looking at the script. The script just gives us hard evidence that this was part of the formation of the character.

I don't think it negates the purpose of editing. Had PTA included this, it would be a very different film, even if you accept what I'm saying about the movie in its current form. I believe there was an intentional distance from this element of the character, but omitting that scene doesn't mean render it absent. It shows itself in subtler ways. The questions about why he doesn't have a wife, which he runs away from, the references to his father, his inexplicable desire for solitude. Removing a scene where a character reveals something about himself is one thing, removing the fact of what he reveals is something else entirely.
2. I love the idea of Daniel as the incarnation of capitalism and it’s obsession with growth at all cost, like a cancer. Him being impotent fits too perfectly with that, given capitalism’s inability to ever create anything new, merely draining the life out of whatever already is.
  - Could you explain what you mean by "capitalism's inability to ever create anything new"?
    - Contrary to the common neoliberal argument that the free market engenders innovation, history shows us that capitalist interest is often opposed to it. A crude version of the steam engine was invented in the 1st century AD, but because the economy of the time was so deeply invested in slavery, the concept was never developed further until slavery started becoming morally untenable  (this is a deeply complex issue and I am simplifying more than I would be comfortable with outside of a Reddit reply). A similar phenomenon can be observed today, with so-called “killer acquisitions,”when powerful tech or pharmaceutical companies buy start-ups or patents that would render their own products obsolete. The medical industry in particular is notorious for funding short-term solutions that are more commercially viable than long-term research that might permanently cure certain ills. On the cultural side, you can see this with the current state of the film industry, with more creative, “riskier” productions getting less and less funding in favor of sequels/prequels/remakes, etc. The impending acquisition of Warner Brothers is only going to lead to fewer films being produced, fewer artists on all levels getting opportunities, movie theaters closing down, etc, while a few guys at the top double their billions. Capitalism does not make innovation happen, it is an Eye of Sauron on the constant lookout for new ideas that it can either prey on or extinguish.
      - Thanks for the response. 
      - Great response!
    - r/badeconomics

Leaving the cinema, I felt it was a tragic love story.  However, I realize this is just my interpretation, and it might not hold up under scrutiny of the film’s facts.

## 3. No Other Choice finds Park Chan-wook at the top of his game

**Score:** 12
**Body:** “No Other Choice” is South Korea’s submission to the best international feature film category at the 2026 Academy Awards, and I’ll tell you right now, it’s a lock for one of the five nomination spots. If you want my predictions, I’ll call it now that the real International Oscar category is a race between “The Secret Agent,” “Sentimental Value,” “It Was Just an Accident,” and “No Other Choice,” with “All That’s Left of You” being a potential dark horse candidate, though that’s definitely more of a long shot than the others. And judging by the way the winds are blowing and the industry vibes I’m privy to, I think it’s really going to come down to “Sentimental Value” vs. “No Other Choice” for the crown.

*// This review is entirely spoiler-free, and also available in* [video form](https://youtu.be/NeTlamkenro) *if you prefer to watch rather than read //*

The great Lee Byung-hun stars as Yoo Man-su, a family man who loses his job in the paper industry after American businessmen get involved and conduct mass layoffs, which leads to the first of many name-drops of the title (this script revels in concocting scenarios for various characters to naturally slip in “I had no other choice”). When trying to get a new job that pays enough to support his family, he faces stiff competition and resorts to essentially becoming a serial killer that specifically targets the other people qualified for the position to make himself the only candidate.

It’s a fun and clever premise that is positively dripping with social commentary. Capitalism is a broken system that will literally drive people to murder. That’s the permanently timely and socially relevant starting point for this concept, and even better, the film isn’t content to sit back and ride that strong central theme all the way to the finish line, instead expanding and digging deeper on that thematic territory. I’m sure many will compare “No Other Choice” to “Parasite” since it’s another South Korean film conveying similar social commentary, and they do both strike a somewhat similar tonal balance between thriller and comedy, but I don’t think there’s much point in comparing them beyond that — except where the Academy is concerned, since “No Other Choice” could well become the second South Korean film to take home an Oscar.

Park Chan-wook was the filmmaker that first sparked my passion for South Korean cinema. Way back in middle school, I came across the “Oldboy” hammer fight out of context on YouTube and thought it was the coolest thing I had ever seen. I watched that one scene over and over again until I was finally able to see the entire film, and when I did, it expanded my adolescent brain’s understanding of what cinema could be. At that point in time, it was the best film I had ever seen (and it still lands extremely high up on my list of [the top 250 best films ever made](https://samkench.com/2025/11/08/top-250-best-films-ever-made/)). I began seeking out and watching any South Korean film I could get my hands on, which of course included all of Park Chan-wook’s other films.

It also included many Lee Byung-hun films, and I’m a huge fan of the guy. His forays into American cinema haven’t fared quite as well, but “I Saw the Devil,” “A Bittersweet Life,” and “The Good, the Bad, and the Weird” are all certified classics in my book. As is “Joint Security Area,” which saw him working with Park Chan-wook for the first time back in 2000. “No Other Choice” is only their second feature film together, though they did collaborate on one of the segments of the “Three… Extremes” horror anthology, with strong results.

I was used to seeing Lee Byung-hun play dramatic, tough, cool characters, so it brought me great delight to see him absolutely nail the comedic elements of this role. There’s still plenty of drama for him to sink his teeth into, and he shines there as usual, but he also especially impressed with killer physical comedy chops and perfectly dialed-in little facial expressions and mannerisms that make moments so much funnier than they would be otherwise. The specific way he slips down some stairs, attempts to walk nonchalantly under pressure, or leans to the side to get the sun out of his eyes are all such brilliant comedic performance details. Just like with Brad Pitt in “Burn After Reading” or Ryan Gosling in “The Nice Guys” and “Barbie,” Lee Byung-hun is another suave leading man that should be allowed to go full goofy more often. “No Other Choice” has a hilarious script to begin with, and Lee Byung-hun’s performance elevates the comedy.

Though it feels deeply rooted in the present day and in South Korean culture, “No Other Choice” is actually an adaptation of an American novel from 1997, which further speaks to the unfortunate universality and timelessness of its themes. That novel is “The Ax” by Donald E. Westlake, which was previously brought to the screen in 2005 by Costa Gavras of “Z” fame. For “No Other Choice,” Park Chan-wook co-wrote the adapted screenplay with a handful of collaborators, including Canadian actor and writer Don McKeller, who also co-created and co-wrote the television series “The Sympathizer” with Park Chan-wook, which is also worth watching, by the way.

“No Other Choice” has a great script that really elevates the fundamentals while streamlining the setup and crafting opportunities for Park Chan-wook to get wild and creative in the director’s chair—there are several moments in the film that aren’t strictly necessary and seem to be in there for no other reason than they’re just good old fashioned fun, and that can be wonderful sometimes. I was particularly impressed by the script’s numerous ingenious set ups and payoffs. Every worthwhile payoff demands a proper setup; that’s a basic tenet of screenwriting, but far too often, either the set up, the payoff, or both are mishandled or telegraphed too obviously. A solid set up that slips past without the audience clocking it as a set up in the moment is oh so satisfying (especially for the screenwriter part of my brain), and “No Other Choice” is chock full of them.

But for as good as the script is, Park Chan-wook is on a whole different plane of existence in terms of directing. A couple of films back, I began referring to him as a cinemagician, as so many of his shots and sequences have the astonishing and beguiling effect of a great magic trick. And nowhere is that more apparent than in “No Other Choice.” Every shot is not only striking and dynamic in varied ways but also cleverly conceived and masterfully executed. There are countless shots and sequences in “No Other Choice” that will leave you scratching your head if you attempt to suss out precisely how they were pulled off. This is the work of a master filmmaker not just excelling at his craft but also taking the time to show off a little while he’s at it.

I still believe “Oldboy” is Park Chan-wook’s best movie overall, but “No Other Choice” does find him at the absolute top of his game as a visual stylist. In this way, I’m reminded of the trajectory of Wes Anderson (odd comparison, I know, but stick with me). “Moonrise Kingdom” and “Rushmore” are my favorite Anderson films, but there’s no denying that works like “Grand Budapest Hotel” or his Roald Dahl short collection display a further refinement of his signature aesthetic and represent the peak of the Wes Anderson style. That’s a similar case to what we have with Park Chan-wook, where I find “Oldboy,” “Sympathy for Mr. Vengeance,” and “The Handmaiden” the stronger overall films, whereas “Decision to Leave” and “No Other Choice” represent the absolute pinnacle of the Park Chan-wook style, even if they don’t quite hit that perfect sweet spot for me that those aforementioned films do.

Now, don’t let me be misunderstood, that’s not meant as any indictment of the script, performances, or anything else in “No Other Choice,” because it is exceptionally strong in all regards. I just get the feeling that there’s just a little something missing—some tiny component that would really tie everything together and leave me calling “No Other Choice” an unabated masterpiece. I’ve had a tough time trying to put my finger on exactly what that is, but it ultimately has to do with the way things are paced and wrap up in the third act. The actual ending is phenomenal, deepening the social commentary and putting the thematic cherry on top, but the immediate lead up to that ending felt just a bit rushed, like certain moments that should have been explored were instead skipped over or streamlined, and like certain plot threads weren’t given enough attention to feel fully earned or resolved. That’s not to say that the third act completely drops the ball or that it isn’t still satisfying, because it is. However, it is really the ONLY aspect of “No Other Choice” that doesn’t live up to the exceedingly high bar set by the rest of the film. I’m curious if a second watch would leave me feeling differently about the third act, and I do find myself wanting to watch it again already.

## 4. How do you interpret the Apathy conversation in Se7en (1995) ?

**Score:** 9
**Body:** >Somerset : This isn’t gonna have a happy ending.

>Mills : If we catch him, I’ll be happy enough.

>S : If we catch him and he turns out to be the devilI …but he’s not the devil. He’s just a man.

>M : see, you bitch and you complain and you tell me these things. If you think you’re preparing me for hard times, thank you, but…

>S : But you gotta be a hero , a champion . Let me tell you, people here don’t want a champion. They wanna eat cheeseburgers and watch TV.

>M : How did you get like this? I wanna know

>S : I just don’t think I can continue to live in a place that nurtures apathy…like a virtue.

>M : You’re no different, no better.

>S : I didn’t say I was different or better .I sympathize .Apathy is a solution.

>It’s easier to lose yourself in drugs than to cope with life..

>It’s easier to steal ……than it is to earn it.

>S : You care?

I view this scene as contrast between moral exhaustion with moral defiance . Somerset sees apathy as a psychological survival strategy while Mills rejects it as surrender , is apathy wisdom or defeat ; but that is just the bare bone and I want to read others deep dive interpretation too

### Top 5 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. That scene is basically realized by the ending of the film, imo. 

Somerset, despite his apathy, finds himself unable to quit the job that drove him to said apathy, as he sees monsters like John Doe and decides he has to be there if someone like that were to ever rise up again (he never verbalizes this but it's what I thought). 

Mills has his hopes and idealism utterly crushed by Doe's murder of his wife and unborn child, to the point he more than likely slips into a deeper apathy than Somerset ever did.
2. The main thing I was thinking during this exchange wasn’t their disagreement, or even who was right. I was thinking about how fundamentally M (Pitt) disagreed with his wife, and that they weren’t talking. This talk needed to happen between them.

As soon as I realized she needed to leave to be who she was, and he needed to stay, and that they loved each other very much, I realized that something terrible was going to happen to them. Either one would have to give up the only principles and goals we’d seen in them, or live a lie, or they would have to split. I couldn’t see the ending coming, but it was clear that their part would be bad.

But as for them, I think M was correct about S, and S knew it, but knew that to think like him would be good for M. That they were incredibly similar, and that S knew becoming like him was Mills’ likely future. So he lied quite carefully to try and not have him make the same self-sacrifice. Not of himself, but of his hope.

Sometimes you make concessions to the world, and Somerset wanted Mills to do that, because he didn’t. And there’s no right answer, just two bad ones to choose from. I think of it when I hear Galadriel’s line about remaining herself, and going into the West. My biggest life choices have been to remain myself and leave things, to be what S wants M to be, because I need hope and happiness. I’m weak.

This film is one of my favourites of all time, along with much lighter fare such as A Matter of Life And Death, or It’s A Wonderful Life, or The Princess Bride. Although I’m a big nerd and watch a lot of nerdshit, Seven and Princess Bride are probably my top two of all time.

And as a card-carrying existentialist (you have to make the card yourself, of course) I see it not as a conversation with a correct answer, but as an open question that you revisit all your life. That you will die with questions like this unanswered, and that you have to live with this.

That the world *might* be a great place, and *might* be worth fighting for.
3. Lots of good comments here, so I’ll add this:

Apart from each character making their philosophical argument for apathy versus caring, it also gives us a deeper respects for Mills, whose crudeness so far has been a sharp contrast to Somerset.

We’ve been led to think Someset is superior. He’s well-read, patient, wise, morally complex. Yet he’s also worn-down, using apathy as an excuse to justify his retirement (which he subconsciously dreads, because it’s a white flag). Someset drapes himself in apathy to morally justify retirement, because he still cares enough to view quitting as surrender, and that he can’t handle. And he really needs an excuse.

Mills is still the cruder man, but his youth and brash naivety awakens something in Someserset, like a child accidentally cheering up an adult simply by being childlike.
4. It’s a great script with characters who are consistent from beginning to end. Mills is dominated by his emotions, Somerset is rational, much more in control of his emotions. The one line from the film which stays with me rent-free is early on, when they’re in that dark stairwell, Mills has one of his dramatic outbursts to which Somerset responds by saying “it’s impressive to see a man feeding off his emotions.”
5. Destruction is easy.  Creation is hard.

John borrows ideas from e.g. Dante and creates his Message to Humanity (about sin, etc.) by destroying individual humans.

John is not apathetic--perhaps he has the opposite problem.  Perhaps he cares too much, cares in an inappropriate way about sin (its causes and effects); and then sins himself, merely reveals himself to be like those he kills.  He invites Mills to destroy him--surely Mills at that moment is not apathetic.

Somerset's claim that apathy is a solution (that some people see it as a solution) is not the same as claiming it is a *good* solution.

## 5. Hatred (2016) best anti-war film?

**Score:** 5
**Body:** When I was a kid, Gone With the Wind was one of my favorite films because I liked how it showed war from a civilian perspective, which was before, during, and after the war. Movies often portray war from the soldiers perspective.

Polish, Yugoslav, and Czechoslovak have good WW2 movies about the occupation of their countries, but many artistically suffered because of government ideological restrictions placed on them in the late 20th century. 

Hatred (or Volhynia) doesn’t suffer from this because it portrays the flawed peace of interwar Poland and the ethnic conflict that happened between Poles, Ukrainians, and Jews during the German and Soviet invasions of Poland. 

It shows the ugly side of what war brings to an occupied population, the hatred and suffering. There’s hardly any soldier heroism, just war crimes from all sides. 

Probably the most brutal war film I seen, even more than Come and See. I’ll recommend watching Hatred.
